meta.note: All cards are drawn from the lecture. Import into Anki/Quizlet as Q/A pairs.

cards.q: What does immunology study?
cards.a: The body's 2nd and 3rd lines of defense.

cards.q: Name the 3 jobs of a healthy immune system.
cards.a: Surveillance, recognition (of foreign material), and destruction of what's foreign.

cards.q: How do white blood cells tell self from non-self?
cards.a: By reading markers on cell surfaces.

cards.q: Which is the ONLY cell in the body without self markers?
cards.a: Red blood cells (erythrocytes) — that's why blood can be transfused.

cards.q: Non-self = ____?
cards.a: Antigen. (The two words mean the same thing.)

cards.q: When do infections actually win?
cards.a: Only when the body is overwhelmed — high stress, a second infection, cancer, or immunosuppression.

cards.q: Define antigen (Ag).
cards.a: Anything foreign (non-self) that triggers an immune response, e.g., an infectious agent.

cards.q: Define antibody (Ab).
cards.a: A protein made by the immune system that binds and inactivates non-self. Also called an immunoglobulin.

cards.q: Define epitope.
cards.a: The specific marker on an antigen's surface that an antibody binds to.

cards.q: What is a titer?
cards.a: A lab measure of antibody amount. Higher titer = more antibodies / more recent exposure.

cards.q: What shape is every antibody, and what do the tips do?
cards.a: A 'Y' shape; the tips of the Y bind the epitopes.

cards.q: What is the MHC?
cards.a: Major Histocompatibility Complex — a set of genes coding cell-surface glycoprotein markers, on all cells except RBCs.

cards.q: How many MHC classes does the professor cover, and which?
cards.a: Only two: Class I and Class II.

cards.q: What does MHC Class I do?
cards.a: Codes the SELF markers placed on every nucleated cell — labels cells as 'us.'

cards.q: What does MHC Class II do?
cards.a: Found on Antigen-Presenting Cells (APCs); presents a piece of a destroyed invader to the T4 helper.

cards.q: Name the antigen-presenting cells (MHC Class II).
cards.a: Macrophages, monocytes, dendritic cells, B lymphocytes, and natural killer cells.

cards.q: Walk through how an APC alerts the immune system.
cards.a: APC engulfs & destroys an invader, displays a piece on its surface; a T4 helper reads it and turns on the immune response.

cards.q: Name the two arms of the immune response and the cells that run them.
cards.a: Humoral (B lymphocytes) and cell-mediated (T lymphocytes).

cards.q: Humoral immunity fights what kind of pathogens?
cards.a: Extracellular: bacteria, toxins, circulating viruses. Weapon = antibodies in serum.

cards.q: Cell-mediated immunity fights what?
cards.a: Intracellular pathogens (viruses, fungi, intracellular bacteria), tumor cells, and grafts. Uses cell contact + cytokines.

cards.q: Where do all lymphocytes start, and how are B and T cells named?
cards.a: Stem cells in bone marrow. B = matures in Bone marrow; T = matures in the Thymus.

cards.q: Name the 3 immunoprivileged sites (graftable without rejection).
cards.a: Upper layers of skin, the lens, and the cornea of the eye.

cards.q: Which WBC responds to BACTERIAL infections?
cards.a: Neutrophils.

cards.q: Which WBC responds to PARASITES and ALLERGIES?
cards.a: Eosinophils.

cards.q: Which WBC increases in VIRAL infections?
cards.a: Lymphocytes.

cards.q: What is diapedesis?
cards.a: When WBCs squeeze out of blood vessels and migrate into tissues to fight infection there.

cards.q: Which two WBCs can both phagocytize AND do diapedesis?
cards.a: Neutrophils and eosinophils.

cards.q: Name the granulocytes vs. agranulocytes.
cards.a: Granulocytes: neutrophils, eosinophils, basophils. Agranulocytes: lymphocytes, monocytes/macrophages.

cards.q: Name the 3 T cells and their one-word roles.
cards.a: T helper (recognition), T cytolytic (inactivation), T suppressor (regulation).

cards.q: What is the single most important immune cell, and why?
cards.a: The T4/CD4 helper — the sentinel; the ONLY cell that can turn on the immune response.

cards.q: Why is HIV/AIDS so dangerous?
cards.a: HIV attacks the CD4 helper — the one cell that switches immunity on — so the whole response can't activate.
CD4. Same cell — 'T4 helper' (professor) and 'CD4' (slides) are the same.

cards.q: What does the T suppressor do after an infection?
cards.a: Regulates/modifies the response — brings T-cell numbers back down once the infection subsides.

cards.q: Give the 5 steps of phagocytosis (mnemonic CAKE).
cards.a: Chemotaxis, Adherence, Ingestion, Killing, Elimination.

cards.q: Phagocytosis step 1: what is chemotaxis?
cards.a: The phagocyte is drawn toward the microbe (attracted by its non-self markers).

cards.q: During 'killing,' what two vesicles fuse and into what?
cards.a: The phagosome + lysosome fuse into a phagolysosome; digestive enzymes destroy the microbe.

cards.q: What happens in the 'elimination' step?
cards.a: Leftover pieces are dumped out, cleaned up by the kidneys, and some excreted in urine.

cards.q: Why are big parasites (worms) attacked from the OUTSIDE?
cards.a: They're too big to engulf, so eosinophils and macrophages attach and attack the surface externally.

cards.q: In the parasite-attack image, what do the blue, red, and yellow spheres represent?
cards.a: Blue = cytotoxic cytokines (recruit help); red = lytic enzymes (break down surface); yellow = perforin (punch holes).

cards.q: What does eosinophilia (high eosinophils) signal?
cards.a: A helminth (parasitic worm) infestation.

cards.q: What are NETs?
cards.a: Neutrophil Extracellular Traps — webs of fibers neutrophils cast to trap and kill microbes (non-phagocytic killing).

cards.q: What are the 3 things complement does (the testable version)?
cards.a: 1) Lyses microbes, 2) neutralizes toxins/viruses, 3) enhances phagocytosis. It's a cascade of proteins.

cards.q: What is the MAC?
cards.a: Membrane Attack Complex — complement proteins that form pores in a pathogen's membrane, bursting it.

cards.q: Why don't your own cells get destroyed by complement?
cards.a: They carry membrane-bound proteins that bind and break down activated complement.

cards.q: Name the 3 complement activation pathways.
cards.a: Classical (pathogen/antibody), Lectin (mannose), Alternative (pathogen/injured tissue; amplifies the others).

cards.q: What do cytokines do?
cards.a: Signal molecules that activate and recruit more immune cells — the immune system's 'text messages for backup.'

cards.q: Cytokines matter to which parts of immunity?
cards.a: Both innate and adaptive immunity.

cards.q: What do B lymphocytes secrete, and what does that make?
cards.a: They secrete plasma cells, which make immunoglobulins (antibodies).

cards.q: Name the 5 antibody classes (mnemonic MADGE).
cards.a: IgM, IgA, IgD, IgG, IgE.

cards.q: IgM — key facts?
cards.a: The 'early bird' — first to appear; too big to cross the placenta; short half-life; agglutinates (clumps) microbes.

cards.q: IgG — key facts?
cards.a: Most important & abundant (~80%); 'for life'; small enough to cross the placenta and give the fetus mom's immunity.

cards.q: IgA — where is it found?
cards.a: In mucous membranes and secretions (saliva, tears, breast milk); guards body surfaces.

cards.q: IgD — key facts?
cards.a: On the surface of B lymphocytes; <1%; role not fully understood; helps initiate the immune response.

cards.q: IgE — key facts?
cards.a: Very rare (~0.002%); the allergy & parasite antibody; triggers mast cells to release histamine; can cause anaphylaxis.

cards.q: What is anaphylaxis, and what drives it?
cards.a: A severe IgE-driven allergic reaction: airway & blood-vessel constriction → hypoxia. Can be fatal; treat with an EpiPen.

cards.q: Primary response: which antibody appears first, and what stays?
cards.a: IgM appears first (early), then fades; IgG rises and stays for life.

cards.q: What is the secondary (anamnestic) response?
cards.a: On re-exposure to the same antigen: a fast, huge IgG burst from memory — you are 'immune.'

cards.q: Does 'immune' mean you'll never get infected again?
cards.a: No — it means a re-infection will be less severe and shorter.

cards.q: Side effect vs. adverse reaction to a vaccine?
cards.a: Side effects (soreness, mild fever) = immune system working normally. Adverse reactions (hives, anaphylaxis, blood clots) are abnormal and rare.

cards.q: Name the 4 types of acquired immunity.
cards.a: Natural active (infection), natural passive (mom/placenta/breast milk), artificial active (vaccine), artificial passive (gamma globulin/plasma).

cards.q: Active vs passive immunity — the difference?
cards.a: Active = your body makes the antibodies (long-term). Passive = you receive ready-made antibodies (short-term).

cards.q: Natural passive immunity — how does a baby get it?
cards.a: Mom's antibodies cross the placenta and come through colostrum/breast milk (short-term).

cards.q: Name 3 artificial passive-immunity agents.
cards.a: Gamma globulin, convalescent plasma, and antivenom/antitoxin — ready-made IgG, short-term.

cards.q: What is gamma globulin?
cards.a: Pooled, concentrated IgG from thousands of donors; short-term (~6 wks–3 mo) passive immunity; works best regionally.

cards.q: How is antivenom (antitoxin) made?
cards.a: Venom is injected into sheep/goats; they make IgG; their filtered plasma (IgG against the venom) is given to the patient.

cards.q: Attenuated vs inactivated vaccine?
cards.a: Attenuated = weakened but ALIVE (Sabin polio; can revert to virulence). Inactivated = dead/inactive (Salk polio; safe).

cards.q: Salk vs Sabin polio vaccine?
cards.a: Salk = inactivated (dead). Sabin = live-attenuated (weakened).

cards.q: Name the 5 vaccine platforms.
cards.a: mRNA/DNA, live-attenuated, inactivated, subunit, and viral-vector.

cards.q: What is a subunit vaccine?
cards.a: Uses only a piece of the microbe (e.g., a protein or flagella) — enough to trigger immunity.

cards.q: What is a viral-vector vaccine?
cards.a: A harmless virus (e.g., adenovirus) delivers the genetic instructions to your cells (J&J, Ebola).

cards.q: What is an adjuvant, and what does it do?
cards.a: An additive (usually a salt) that causes mild irritation → recruits more WBCs → a stronger immune response.

cards.q: What is herd immunity?
cards.a: When ~70–80% of a population is immune, the pathogen can't spread — protecting even the unvaccinated.

cards.q: What should a good vaccine do?
cards.a: Low toxicity, protect vs the wild pathogen, trigger BOTH B and T responses, long-term memory, few boosters, cheap/stable/easy to give.

cards.q: What is thimerosal, and is it linked to autism?
cards.a: A mercury preservative that stabilizes proteins in multi-dose vials. No — the autism claim was a fraudulent, retracted study.
